title: The Perfect Tense — Talking About the Past
description: This is how Germans talk about the past in everyday speech. It's built from two pieces: a helper verb (haben or sein) plus a past participle parked at the end of the sentence.

lessons.title: How the Perfect Tense Works
lessons.subtitle: haben + past participle — the everyday past.
lessons.hook: In conversation, Germans rarely use the simple past for most verbs — they use the Perfect tense. 'I have played', 'I have eaten', 'I have worked'. It sounds like English present perfect, but Germans use it to mean plain past too. Master its two-piece structure and you can suddenly talk about yesterday.
lessons.explain: The Perfect tense = a conjugated helper verb (usually haben) + a past participle at the END of the sentence. For most verbs the participle is formed ge- + stem + -t (machen → gemacht, spielen → gespielt). Example: 'Ich habe Fußball gespielt' (I played football). The helper 'haben' sits in position 2 and carries the conjugation; the participle 'gespielt' waits at the very end — the same 'action-verb-goes-last' rhythm you met with modals.

vocabulary.memoryHook: machen → gemacht (ge + mach + t). The model regular participle.
arbeiten → gearbeitet (stem ends in -t, so add -et).
Time word that signals the past — pairs naturally with the Perfect.

exercises.prompt: The Perfect tense is built from…
options: a helper verb (haben/sein) + a past participle
exercises.correctAnswer: a helper verb (haben/sein) + a past participle
exercises.explanation: Perfect = conjugated haben (or sein) in position 2, plus the past participle at the end of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: What is the past participle of 'machen'?
exercises.explanation: Regular participles follow ge- + stem + -t: machen → gemacht. Same pattern: spielen → gespielt, lernen → gelernt.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich habe Fußball ___.' (I played football.) — verb: spielen
exercises.hint: ge + spiel + t, and it goes at the end.
exercises.explanation: spielen → gespielt. The participle sits at the end: 'Ich habe Fußball gespielt.'

lessons.title: sein as Helper + Irregular Participles
lessons.subtitle: Movement and change use 'sein'; strong verbs end in -en.
lessons.hook: Most verbs build the Perfect with haben — but a special group uses sein instead: verbs of movement (gehen, fahren, kommen) and change of state (aufstehen, werden). 'Ich bin nach Berlin gefahren' — I drove to Berlin. Knowing which helper to grab is the last piece of talking naturally about the past.
lessons.explain: Use sein (not haben) as the helper for verbs that show movement from A to B (gehen, kommen, fahren, fliegen) or a change of state (aufstehen, einschlafen, werden). These are usually intransitive (no direct object). Example: 'Ich bin nach Hause gegangen'. Also, many common verbs are 'strong' (irregular): their participle ends in -en, often with a vowel change — gehen → gegangen, fahren → gefahren, essen → gegessen, trinken → getrunken, sehen → gesehen. These simply must be memorized, but the most useful ones are a short, high-value list.

vocabulary.memoryHook: gehen → gegangen. Uses SEIN (movement). Strong: ends in -en.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Er ist mit dem Auto gefahren.

vocabulary.memoryHook: essen → gegessen. Has an object → HABEN. Irregular double-g.
Separable + change of state → SEIN. Participle: aufgestanden (ge sits inside).

exercises.prompt: Which helper verb do movement verbs (gehen, fahren, kommen) use in the Perfect?
exercises.explanation: Verbs of movement (A→B) and change of state use 'sein': 'Ich bin gefahren / gekommen / gegangen.' Most other verbs use 'haben'.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Ich ___ nach Hause gegangen.' (I went home.)
exercises.hint: gehen is movement → sein; 'ich' form of sein.
exercises.explanation: gehen uses 'sein', and the 'ich' form of sein is 'bin': 'Ich bin nach Hause gegangen.'

exercises.prompt: Which participle is correct for 'essen' (to eat)?
exercises.explanation: essen is a strong verb: essen → gegessen (ends in -en, with a doubled g). It takes 'haben' because it has a direct object.
